items.correct: Proteins denaturing and bonding to form a solid structure when heated.
items.misconception: The egg shell breaking when boiled.
items.why: Students think coagulation is something physical happening to the shell. It's PROTEIN CHEMISTRY: heat unfolds the proteins, which then bond together, turning liquid egg into solid - what makes a fried egg set.

items.q: What temperature range is the food 'danger zone'?
items.correct: 5 to 63 degrees Celsius - where most bacteria multiply rapidly.
items.why: Students assume danger = very hot. It's the MIDDLE range that's dangerous because bacteria thrive there. Fridges stay below 5; cooking exceeds 63 (often 75+ for safety). The middle is where they multiply.

items.q: What is the purpose of a star profile?
items.correct: Comparing multiple sensory attributes of products visually on a radar diagram.
items.misconception: Awarding overall scores out of 5 stars.
items.why: Students confuse star profiles with rating systems. Star profiles plot attributes (sweetness, crunch, saltiness, etc.) on radial axes - the SHAPE shows the product's sensory fingerprint, not its quality rating.

items.q: An egg setting solid when cooked is.
items.why: It's Coagulation. “Gelatinisation” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
The answer examiners want is: Maillard reaction. “Caramelisation” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Roughly what temperature does Maillard reaction start (C)?
items.why: Exact answer: 140. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
